content.text: The critical path of a GenAI initiative is the longest sequence of dependent tasks from project start to end. Any task on the critical path has
— if it slips even one week, the project end date slips by one week. Tasks not on the critical path have
(slack) — they can be delayed by their float amount without affecting the end date. Understanding the critical path tells a programme manager exactly where to focus risk management attention.
The critical path is not fixed. If a non-critical task slips beyond its float, it becomes critical. Re-compute the critical path whenever task durations or dependencies change.

In the example above, the critical path is T1 → T3 → T5 → T7 (11 weeks total). The data pipeline must finish by Week 3 for model training to begin; training must finish by Week 7 for the security review to start; security review must finish by Week 9 for production deployment to begin. T2 (Infrastructure) has 2 weeks of float — it can start up to Week 2 without delaying T7.
Computing Earliest Start and Finish (Forward Pass)
Critical path analysis uses two passes through the task graph. The
computes the Earliest Start (ES) and Earliest Finish (EF) for every task, working left to right from project start:
works right to left from the project end date, computing the Latest Start (LS) and Latest Finish (LF) — the last moment each task can start or finish without delaying the project:
Both passes require tasks in dependency order. The standard algorithm is Kahn's algorithm (BFS-based topological sort):
Risk Management for Critical Path Tasks
Once the critical path is identified, risk management effort concentrates there. Three practices protect critical-path tasks:
Add explicit schedule buffers (contingency reserves) to uncertain critical-path tasks. The standard PERT approach estimates three durations:

def pert_expected_duration(optimistic: float, most_likely: float, pessimistic: float) -> float:
    """PERT formula: (O + 4M + P) / 6"""
    return (optimistic + 4 * most_likely + pessimistic) / 6

def pert_std_dev(optimistic: float, pessimistic: float) -> float:
    return (pessimistic - optimistic) / 6

# Example: model training with uncertain convergence
training_duration = pert_expected_duration(3, 4, 7)   # 4.17 weeks
training_std_dev  = pert_std_dev(3, 7)                # 0.67 weeks
# Add 1 std_dev as buffer: plan for 4.84 weeks
# Two sigma (95 % confidence): plan for 5.5 weeks
print(f"Expected: {training_duration:.1f}w  Buffer (+1σ): {training_duration + training_std_dev:.1f}w")

Define explicit triggers that activate escalation before a task actually misses its end date. A task with a 4-week duration that has consumed 3 weeks and completed only 50 % of its scope is clearly at risk — waiting for the deadline to escalate is too late.
Non-critical tasks that consume their float become critical. Track float consumption weekly for tasks that are near-critical (float ≤ 2 weeks):
Critical Path in Practice for GenAI Programmes

Formal critical path analysis using durations and float is most valuable for multi-team initiatives with hard delivery dates (regulatory deadlines, product launch commitments). For exploratory or research-heavy initiatives, the critical path shifts frequently as experiments resolve unknowns — in those cases, a lighter approach (weekly dependency check rather than formal CPM) is more appropriate.
Full CPM with float analysis; PERT durations for uncertain tasks
Weekly for critical-path tasks; biweekly for others
Full CPM; legal/security review tasks always on critical path
Informal dependency order only; no float analysis needed
Full CPM for integration milestones; PoC tasks lighter
: a task slips; the critical path shifts; but the team is still managing the old critical path. Re-analyse after every significant duration change.
: vendor contracts, legal reviews, and procurement approvals are tasks with durations. They must be in the task graph, not treated as background activities.
: model training convergence, dataset labelling quality, and prompt engineering iteration are inherently variable. Use PERT ranges; do not use single-point estimates.
: near-critical tasks (float ≤ 2 weeks) deserve attention too — they are one slip away from becoming critical.
PERT-Based Duration Estimation for Critical Tasks
Program Expected Review Time (PERT) replaces single-point duration guesses with a three-point estimate:
. This is especially useful for GenAI tasks where variance is high—model evaluations, vendor API integrations, and data clearance reviews can slip significantly under real conditions.

Importantly, you cannot simply add individual task uncertainties in the tightest sense: when tasks overlap or parallelise, the critical path PERT sum still applies to the sequential portion only. For programme planning, feed PERT expected durations—not most-likely durations—into your CPM network so that the schedule already bakes in a statistical mean rather than an optimistic guess.
If the 95% confidence interval width exceeds

), the task carries too much schedule risk to sit silently on the critical path. Mandatory actions: time-box a discovery spike (1–2 weeks), run a pre-mortem on the pessimistic scenario, and surface the range to programme sponsors rather than quoting only the expected value.
Float Buffer Policies and Escalation Runbook

Total float indicates how much schedule slack a non-critical task has before it becomes critical. A well-run GenAI programme defines formal policies around float levels so that schedule slippage is caught and escalated before it collapses the critical path.
Flag in weekly standup; identify recovery options
Escalate to programme board; trigger contingency
Immediate re-plan; all hands on deck

Integrate this evaluation into your weekly programme reporting pipeline. On every run, the function fires the appropriate notification channel (Slack, email, Jira comment) automatically so that escalation is never dependent on a human remembering to check a spreadsheet.
Two common buffer patterns for GenAI programmes:

: add a buffer activity after each high-PERT-variance critical task (e.g., 20% of expected duration). The buffer is deliberately identified so it can be consumed without impacting the baseline schedule—and its consumption is tracked as an early warning signal.

: strip contingency from individual tasks and pool it as a single project buffer at the end of the critical path. Programme managers draw from the pool deliberately and report buffer consumption (0–33% green, 34–66% amber, 67–100% red) rather than task-level dates.

Critical path analysis identifies the longest dependency chain in a GenAI initiative — the sequence where any slip directly extends the project end date. The forward pass computes earliest start/finish for each task; the backward pass computes latest start/finish; float (LS − ES) identifies which tasks are critical (float = 0) and which have slack. PERT three-point estimates handle uncertain task durations. Risk management concentrates on critical-path and near-critical tasks through duration buffers, early-warning progress triggers, and weekly float consumption monitoring. For multi-team GenAI initiatives with hard milestones, formal CPM analysis is worth the setup cost; for exploratory work, a lighter biweekly dependency check suffices.
